coredata.dc:title: The effects of various molecular weight of passivator on the photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xingqun
Li, Zhan
Hu, Guojing
Li, Jing
Xiang, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Here, we realize manipulation of photoluminescence (PL) intensity of GQDs through various molecular weight of surface passivator. With the increase of passivator molecular weight, only the PL intensity increases. However, the peak positions and half-peak width of spectral line remain unchanged. Our results reveal that surface passivation with larger molecular weight only weakens the chemical activity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the π-π stacking interaction between GQDs, but exerts less impact on GQDs intrinsic energy gap. Therefore, the weaker π-π stacking interaction and chemical activity induce the weaker non-radiative recombination, leading to enhanced fluorescence from radiative recombination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Passivator
Graphene
Quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Molecular weight

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of different molecular weight polymer passivated GQDs
The preparation of PEG passivated GQDs
The preparation of PVP passivated GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of different molecular weight polymer passivated GQDs.

(a) PL spectra of different molecular weight PEG passivated GQDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength. (b) The corresponding PL excitation (PLE) spectrum of different molecular weight PEG passivated GQDs with fixed emission wavelength at 425 nm. (c) UV–visible absorption of different molecular weight PEG passivated GQDs.

(a) PL spectra of different molecular weight PVP passivated GQDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength, and the corresponding PL excitation (PLE) spectrum of different molecular weight PVP passivated GQDs with fixed emission wavelength at 437 nm. (b) UV–visible absorption of different molecular weight PEG passivated GQDs. (c) PL emission spectra of PVP K17 passivated GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 400 nm, (d) PL emission spectra of PVP K25 passivated GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 400 nm, (e) PL emission spectra of PVP K30 passivated GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 400 nm, (f) the excitation dependent PL peak locations PVP passivated GQDs extracted from (c) (d) and (e).
(a) (d) The TEM image of PVP K17 passivated GQDs, (b) (e) The TEM image of PVP K25 passivated GQDs, (c) (f) The TEM image of PVP K25 passivated GQDs.
FTIR spectrum of (a) different molecular weight PEG passivated GQDs, and (b) different molecular weight PVP passivated GQDs.
Mechanism of fluorescence changes in graphene quantum dots passivated by polymers of different molecular weights.

head.ce:title: The effects of various molecular weight of passivator on the photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photonic Technology Research and Development Center, Quanzhou Normal University, No 398, Donghai Main Street, Quanzhou, 362000, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Photonic Technology Research and Development Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, CAS Key Lab of Materials for Energy Conversion, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, Anhui, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Photonic Technology Research and Development Center, Quanzhou Normal University, No 398, Donghai Main Street, Quanzhou, 362000, Fujian, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Photonic Technology Research and Development Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we realize manipulation of photoluminescence (PL) intensity of GQDs through various molecular weight of surface passivator. With the increase of passivator molecular weight, only the PL intensity increases. However, the peak positions and half-peak width of spectral line remain unchanged. Our results reveal that surface passivation with larger molecular weight only weakens the chemical activity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the π-π stacking interaction between GQDs, but exerts less impact on GQDs intrinsic energy gap. Therefore, the weaker π-π stacking interaction and chemical activity induce the weaker non-radiative recombination, leading to enhanced fluorescence from radiative recombination.

ce:section.ce:para: Since graphene was successfully separated by micromechanical peeling in 2004 [1], it has attracted extensive attention due to many superior properties, such as giant carrier mobility, high thermal conductivity, and stiffness [2]. Graphene and graphene oxides have been used in the field of solar cells [3–7], sensors [8], and batteries [9]. However, as the band gap of graphene is zero, it is impossible for us to observe its luminescence, which limits its application in optics. Therefore, many different strategies are used to open the band gap of graphene, such as doping [10,11], treatment of graphene oxide (GO) via surface passivation, and partial reduction [12,13]. In addition, cleaving two-dimensional graphene into zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with 10 nm size is also one of the most important strategies. GQDs have similar properties with other 0D colloid quantum dots, like the quantum size effect, quantum tunneling effect, quantum confinement effect, and surface effect [14,15]. One of the most intriguing features of GQDs is photoluminescence (PL). Due to its low toxicity and biocompatibility, GQDs are widely used in the fields of biological imaging, biosensors, and drug transport and release [16–18]. In addition, some researchers apply GQDs in other fields such as photocatalysis [19], secondary batteries [20], and light-emitting diodes [21].

At present, various synthesis methods of GQDs have been widely proposed, which are mainly classified into two categories: top-down method and bottom-up strategy [22,23]. The top-down method is generally operated by means of a series of oxidation cutting and crushing of larger carbon sources. During the synthesis process of GQDs, surface passivation plays an important role to obtain great fluorescence property. If the surface of GQDs is not modified or passivated, they will face severe agglomeration and stacking due to the size and surface effects, which can weaken the luminescence performance, and decrease PL quantum yield (QY). However, its fluorescence performance can be greatly improved after the passivation. Li's group [24] prepared strong blue emission GQDs with high upconverted PL through hydrazine hydrate reduction GO with PEG1500N as surface passivation in 2011. Tang et al. [25] synthesized water-soluble self-passivated GQDs showing deep ultraviolet emission by using glucose as a carbon source through a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Hence, the selection of passivator is a key factor to improve the PL of GQDs. Here, we select two different kinds of polymer as surface passivating agents, and investigate the effects of molecular weight of passivators on the fluorescence properties of GQDs. We conducted a series of characterizations, such as fluorescence spectrum, Uv–vis absorption spectrum, TEM, FTIR. As far as we know, the previous reports mainly focused on the usage of polymer passivator can significantly improve fluorescent properties of graphene quantum dots. However, they did not focus on that passivator with different molecular weight has different effect on changing the fluorescence intensity. Here, our work reveals that the PL intensity of GQDs can be increased by the increasing of molecular weight of polymer passivator, and we also put forward the mechanism to explain this.

GO was from the Sixth Element (Changzhou) Materials Technology Co. Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamidel (DMF), Polyethylene glycol (PEG 1000, 4000, 6000), and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP–K17, K25, K30) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used directly without further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of different molecular weight polymer passivated GQDs
The preparation of PEG passivated GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL GO solution (20 mg/mL) was added to a three-neck flask, which contains 120 mL of nitric acid solution (60 mL HNO3 and 60 mL H2O). Then the solution was backflow stirred under 70 °C for 36 h, and subsequently transferred to the beaker. Finally, water was added into the solution to 250 mL. The obtained solution was ultrasonicated in the ice bath for 3 h, and then stood for 3 h. After that, the supernatant fluid was poured out. Whereafter, DMF was added to 250 mL. The mixed solution was ultrasonicated for 2 h, and then transferred to the reaction kettle. The same amount (3 g) of PEG with different molecular weight (PEG1000, PEG4000, PEG6000) were added to the different reaction kettle, respectively. The reaction kettle was heated up to 200 °C and kept for 36 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm-size microporous membrane. Finally, the clarified fluorescent quantum dots solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of PVP passivated GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL GO solution (20 mg/mL) was added to the 250 mL of ethanol solution (150 mL ethanol and 100 mL H2O). The obtained mixed solution was ultrasonicated in the ice bath for 2 h, and then transferred to the reaction kettle. The same amount (0.5 g) of PVP with different molecular weight (K17, K25, K30) were added to the different reaction kettle, respectively. Afterwards, the reaction kettle was heated up to 180 °C and kept for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm-size microporous membrane. Finally, the clarified fluorescent quantum dots solution was obtained.

The preparation process of GQDs was performed on ultrasonic homogenizer (Ningbo Scientz Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Zhejiang, China) and blast drying box (Shanghai Jinghong experimental equipment Co., Ltd., DHG-9036A). The PL spectrum was detected by JY Fluorolog-3-Tou spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon, France). Uv–visible absorption spectra (Uv–vis) were obtained on P4 Uv–visible spectrophotometer (MAPADA, Shanghai). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic measurements were carried out on Nicolet 8700 Fourier-transform spectrometer using KBr pressed disks (Thermo Nicolet, America). TEM images were obtained from field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM) (JEM-2100F, Japan).

To further rule out the contingency, the fluorescence properties of different molecular weight PVP as passivators are demonstrated. Fig. 3 a displays the PL spectra of different molecular weight PVP passivated GQDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The corresponding PLE spectrum with fixed emission wavelength is at 437 nm. In the three PL curves, the strongest peak appears at 437 nm under the excitation of 360 nm, which shows a corresponding Stokes shift of 77 nm. In addition, the PL intensity of corresponding GQDs become stronger with the molecular weight of PVP increases from K17 to K30. GQDs were induced to emit strongest emission peak of 437 nm with the excitation wavelength which is located at ca.326 nm. In Fig. 3b, the UV–visible absorption spectrum shows that the GQDs passivated by PVP with different molecular weights have similar absorption curves, including absorption peaks and absorption edges. Inset of Fig. 3b is a photograph of the PVP passivated GQDs under UV light with 365 nm excitation. The GQDs emit strong blue luminescence and have a visible to the naked eye increased intensity of light with the PVP changed from K17 to K30. Fig. 3c–e displays PL emission spectra of PVP K17, PVP K25, and PVP K30 passivated GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 400 nm, respectively. It can be can clearly seen that all the PL spectrums are broad and dependent on the excitation wavelength. The strongest PL peak location has red shift as the excitation wavelength changes from 320 to 400 nm. According to the excitation dependent PL peak locations of three PVP passivated GQDs extracted from Fig. 3c, d, and 3e, the relationship between excitation wavelength and peak position is fitted. And it can be found that the excitation-wavelength is linearly dependent with the PL behavior, which is similar to some previous works [26,27]. In addition, three PVP passivated GQDs display the same trend. Table S1 compares the PL QY of GQDs passivated by different polymer. In PEG passivated GQDs, the PL QY is 0.69%, 1.68%, and 2.81% for PEG 1000, 4000, and 6000, respectively. As for PVP passivated GQDs, the PL QY is 2.85%, 3.54%, and 5.40% for PVP 17, PVP 25, and PVP 30, respectively. Obviously, it is found that the PL QY increases with the increasing of molecular weight. Since many factors may influence the fluorescent properties of GQDs, such as size, pH, etc [28–30], the experimental conditions are kept the same except molecular weight of passivator. Thus, it is needed to further validate that the different fluorescence intensity is not arisen from the size of GQDs. Fig. 4 compares the size of GQDs that are passivated by different molecular weight PVP. Fig. 4a and d are the TEM image of PVP K17 passivated GQDs. Fig. 4b and e shows the TEM image of PVP K25 passivated GQDs. And the TEM image of PVP K30 passivated GQDs are exhibited in Fig. 4c and f. To the naked eye, the sizes of the three GQDs are basically the same. The size distribution of three GQDs are calculated, and shown in the inset image of Fig. 4d, e, and 4f, respectively. According to results, it is known that the sizes of GQDs passivated by all three PVP are mainly distributed in ca. 2 nm (1.67 nm size accounts for the largest proportion). In fact, the sizes of three PVP passivated GQDs are mainly distributed in 1–3 nm, which indicates that there is no obvious difference in size. Therefore, it can be confirmed that polymer surface passivator will not significantly change the real size of quantum dots, but it will affect the state of quantum dots in colloidal solution. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results reflect the real state of quantum dots in colloid. As shown in Fig. S1, the hydrodynamic diameter of PVP passivated GQDs increases with the increasing of molecular weight from K17–K30. The diameter determined by DLS is much larger than the real size of quantum dots, which is mainly due to the interaction between GQDs and polymer PVP molecule. The surface of quantum dots connects with long-chain groups, which shows on the TEM images is that the organic ingredients on the substrate increases with the increasing of molecular weight. In addition, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs passivated by various molecular weights are not affected by their size.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterization was conducted to identify chemical groups in GQDs modified by different molecular weight passivation agent. Fig. 5 a shows the FTIR of GQDs. A broad band at 3420 cm−1 is assigned to –OH groups [31]. The peaks at 2885 cm−1 are derived from the -C-H stretching vibration. Two representative peaks located at 1343 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are assigned to –NO2 group, which is derived from the nitric acid oxidation [23]. The stretching vibration peaks of -C-O are observed at 962 and 1112 cm−1. In Fig. 5b, the broad representative peak of –OH groups is observed at 3435 cm−1. The peaks at 1655 cm−1 can be attributed to –CO groups [31,32]. The Ar (aryl)-H out-of-plane bending vibrational mode (two adjacent hydrogens) is also observed at 833 cm−1. The peaks appearing at 1293 cm−1 are the -C-C stretching vibration mode. The GQDs modified by the same passivator possess almost the same spectral line shapes and peak positions. The peak position has no change, which means that the types of functional groups remain the same. The number of functional groups cannot be quantitatively reflected by the FTIR data measured by KBr pressed disks method, but it can be reflected by other data. These results show that the fluorescence intensity of polymer-passivated GQDs can be modified by varying the molecular weight of passivation agent. To explain this, the role of the passivator is needed to be understood. Surface passivation is similar to surface functionalization, which can improve the performance of quantum dots. Due to the small size and the lack adjacent atoms around surface of quantum dots, many dangling bonds are unsaturated, which results in high chemical activity, thus it is easy to combine with surrounding atoms to reach stable. In addition, on account of the small size of quantum dots, the surface area, surface energy, and surface binding energy are relatively large (surface effect). Such a large specific surface area means that there may be large number of lattice defects on the surface of quantum dots, thus increasing the ratio of non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination and reducing the fluorescence efficiency. Passivation makes the surface of quantum dots surrounded by a variety of functional groups, which can alleviate the stack interaction between 0D quantum dots, and inhibit the non-radiative recombination process of graphene quantum dots caused by π-π stacking. As shown in Fig. 6 , the mechanism of fluorescence changes in GQDs passivated by polymers of different molecular weight is proposed. The chain length of polymers increases with the increases of molecular weight. Thanks to the attachment of longer-chain passivator on the surface of GQDs, the non-radiative recombination which is induced by the interaction between 0D GQDs can be better suppressed, thus improving the radiative luminescence.

In summary, various molecular weight polymer passivated GQDs are successfully prepared by ultrasonic assisted hydrothermal method, and their fluorescence properties are studied in detail. The PL intensity of polymer passivated GQDs gradually increases with the rising of molecular weight of passivation agent. The almost identical spectral line shapes and same peak locations are showed in the same passivator modified GQDs though the molecular weight is different. Such a phenomenon is mainly due to the larger molecular weight of the passivator, which can better inhibit the non-radiative recombination between GQDs, thus improving the fluorescence efficiency.

Xingqun Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Zhan Li: Data curation, Resources. Guojing Hu: Writing - original draft. Jing Li: Supervision. Bin Xiang: Data curation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Fujian provincial development and reform commission investment [2017] No 436.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of large, stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size
